shortDescription.text: Path parameter injection enables SSRF via URL manipulation

fullDescription.text: User-controlled path parameters are interpolated directly into the server URL using string replacement (url.replace) without URL-encoding or validation. An attacker can inject values like '../', '@attacker.com', or '#' to manipulate the target URL, potentially bypassing the ssrf_proxy protections. For example, a path parameter value of '../../internal-service' or 'ignored@evil.com:443/' could redirect the request to an internal or attacker-controlled host.

shortDescription.text: follow_redirects=True allows SSRF via open redirect chains

fullDescription.text: HTTP requests are made with follow_redirects=True, which means even if the initial URL passes ssrf_proxy validation, the response could redirect to an internal/metadata endpoint (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/). This is a classic SSRF bypass technique: the attacker hosts a redirect on an allowed external host that points to an internal resource.

shortDescription.text: User-controlled values injected into HTTP headers without sanitization

fullDescription.text: Parameters with 'in: header' from the OpenAPI spec are directly set as HTTP headers with only str() conversion. No CRLF filtering or character validation is performed at the application layer. While httpx may reject malformed headers, defense-in-depth requires input sanitization.

shortDescription.text: HTTP response body leaked in error messages

fullDescription.text: When the external API returns a 4xx/5xx status, the full response body (response.text) is included in the ToolInvokeError exception message. This may expose internal API error details, stack traces, or sensitive data from the target service to the end user or logs.

shortDescription.text: User-controlled values set as HTTP cookies without validation

fullDescription.text: Parameters with 'in: cookie' from the OpenAPI spec are directly assigned to the cookies dict from user-supplied input without any sanitization or validation. An attacker could inject specially crafted cookie values to perform session fixation or manipulate server-side cookie parsing.

shortDescription.text: User-provided string values parsed as JSON objects/arrays without schema validation

fullDescription.text: In _convert_body_property_type, when property type is 'object' or 'array', string values are parsed via json.loads() without any schema validation or depth limit. A deeply nested or excessively large JSON payload could cause resource exhaustion in downstream processing.

shortDescription.text: Timeout values controllable via environment variables without upper bound

fullDescription.text: API_TOOL_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT connect and read timeout values are read from environment variables and cast to int without upper bound validation. A misconfigured environment could set extremely high timeouts enabling resource exhaustion.

message.text: Path parameters are interpolated into the URL via `url.replace(f"{{{name}}}", f"{value}")` without URL-encoding or host validation. An attacker supplying a path parameter like `..%2F..%2Finternal` or `ignored@attacker.com` can alter the target host/path, potentially bypassing ssrf_proxy's initial URL check. The value should be URL-encoded with `urllib.parse.quote(str(value), safe='')` and the resulting URL re-validated against an allowlist.

HTTP requests use `follow_redirects=True`, allowing an attacker to bypass ssrf_proxy validation by having the allowed external URL respond with a 3xx redirect to an internal endpoint (e.g., cloud metadata at http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). The ssrf_proxy must validate each redirect target, not just the initial URL. If ssrf_proxy does not re-validate on redirect, this is a critical SSRF bypass.

User-supplied parameter values are set as HTTP headers via `headers[parameter["name"]] = str(value)` when the OpenAPI spec declares `in: header`. No CRLF filtering or character validation is performed at the application layer. While httpx may reject malformed headers, defense-in-depth requires sanitizing header values (stripping \r, \n, \0) before passing them to the HTTP client.

The full HTTP response body is included in the ToolInvokeError when status >= 400: `f"Request failed with status code {response.status_code} and {response.text}"`. The remote API's error response may contain internal details (stack traces, database errors, internal paths) that should not be exposed to end users. Truncate or sanitize `response.text` before including it in user-facing error messages.

User-supplied parameter values are passed directly as HTTP cookies via `cookies[parameter["name"]] = value` when the OpenAPI spec declares `in: cookie`. No validation or sanitization is performed on cookie names or values. An attacker could inject specially crafted cookie values to perform session fixation or exploit cookie parsing inconsistencies on the target server.

In `_convert_body_property_type`, when property type is 'object' or 'array', user-supplied string values are parsed via `json.loads(value)` without schema validation, depth limits, or size constraints. While json.loads is not vulnerable to code execution, a deeply nested or extremely large JSON payload could cause resource exhaustion in downstream processing. Consider adding size limits and schema validation.

Timeout values are read from environment variables `API_TOOL_DEFAULT_CONNECT_TIMEOUT` and `API_TOOL_DEFAULT_READ_TIMEOUT` with defaults of 10s and 60s respectively, but no upper bound is enforced. A compromised or misconfigured environment could set extremely high values, causing worker threads to block indefinitely on slow external endpoints, leading to resource exhaustion and denial of service.
